label: Dartmouth Workshop
definition: The 1956 summer meeting at Dartmouth College, organized by John McCarthy and others, that named the field of Artificial Intelligence and launched it as an organized research discipline. It is conventionally treated as the birth of AI.

body.text: The Dartmouth Workshop was a roughly eight-week gathering at Dartmouth College in the summer of 1956, formally titled the Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence. It was organized by John McCarthy, then a young mathematician at Dartmouth, together with Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester of IBM, and Claude Shannon of Bell Labs, and it brought a small group of researchers together to spend the summer attacking the problem of making machines think. The workshop is conventionally treated as the founding event of Artificial Intelligence as a named field, and its proposal is where the term itself was first put into print.

The proposal that secured the funding is a historical document in its own right, both for its ambition and for its famous optimism. It opened with the conjecture that "every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it," and it proposed that a carefully selected group could make significant progress on this in a single summer working together. McCarthy chose the phrase "artificial intelligence" deliberately, in part to stake out a field distinct from the established but narrower banner of cybernetics and from the specific commitments of those already working on neural nets and automata. The name stuck, and it has defined the discipline ever since.

The workshop matters less for any single result it produced, it generated no breakthrough theorem or system on the spot, than for what it consolidated. It assembled the people who would lead the field for the next generation, McCarthy and Minsky above all, who went on to found the AI laboratories at Stanford and MIT, alongside Allen Newell and Herbert Simon, who arrived with the Logic Theorist, an early program that proved theorems in symbolic logic and is often cited as the first working AI program. The meeting set the agenda, the vocabulary, and the social network of a new research community, turning scattered efforts into a coherent enterprise with a shared name and shared questions.

The intellectual stance that crystallized at Dartmouth shaped AI for decades and helps explain its later history. The dominant conviction was that intelligence could be achieved by manipulating symbols according to explicit rules, the program now called Symbolic AI or, retrospectively, good old-fashioned AI. This top-down, logic-and-search approach drove the Expert System era and produced real successes, but its confident timelines went unmet, and the gap between the founders' optimism and what the technology could actually deliver contributed to the disillusionment and funding collapse of the first AI Winter. The rival, bottom-up tradition of learning from data, rooted in the Perceptron and Connectionism, was present at the edges in 1956 but would not become dominant until the deep learning era many decades later.

The Dartmouth Workshop endures as the symbolic origin point of the field this site tracks, the moment AI acquired its name and its institutional identity. Reading its proposal today is instructive precisely because of the distance between its framing and the modern reality: the founders bet on hand-built symbolic reasoning and underestimated perception and learning, almost the exact inversion of which problems turned out to be hard, the pattern later named in Moravec's paradox. The workshop is best remembered not as a moment of solved problems but as the founding act of a discipline that has been relearning the limits of its founding assumptions ever since.
